task: I’m trying to make a short school article about a game, but I only have a few notes and I’m not sure which parts are solid enough to include. Can you help me sort out what I can say confidently and what I should leave out until I check it?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s the simplest way to sort it:

## Safe to include now
- It was **a game played on a Tuesday afternoon**.
- You can mention it was **a school sports game**.
- If your notes say the team **played hard / had issues with spacing, passing, and reacting fast enough**, that can be included **if it’s clearly your observation**.

## Not safe yet
- **The date** — leave it out until you verify it.
- **The final score** — you have a report of **3-1**, but it’s **not cross-checked**, so treat it as unverified.
- Any exact details about **who scored, where it was played, or final outcome wording** if you haven’t confirmed them.

## Best rule for your article
Only write as fact what you can **confirm from more than one source or from reliable notes**.

## Easy skeleton you can use
**On [day/time], [team name] played [opponent] in a school game. The team [general observation about how they played]. The result was [confirmed score] if verified.**

If you want, I can turn your notes into a **fill-in-the-blank article template** with only the parts you can safely use right now.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, but I’d want it a little stricter than that. If I didn’t verify it, I wouldn’t put it in as fact at all — even stuff like the day or the score if I only have one note for it.

What I’d keep:
- stuff I actually saw
- stuff I heard from a reliable source and checked
- general observations, like if the team looked rushed or missed passes

What I’d leave out for now:
- exact score unless I confirmed it
- names of players who scored unless I’m sure
- anything that sounds like guessing

Also, if this is for school newspaper style, I’d rather sound plain than try to make it dramatic. Like, just say what happened and leave the rest until I can check it. If you want, I can help turn the notes into a version that only uses confirmed details.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — that stricter approach is the right one for school newspaper style.

## Use only what you can confirm
**Safe to include**
- things you personally saw
- details from a reliable source that you checked
- general, clearly observed impressions like:
  - the team looked rushed
  - passes were missed
  - spacing was off

**Leave out for now**
- exact score unless you confirmed it
- player names unless you’re sure
- the day/time if it’s only from one shaky note
- anything that feels like guessing

## Simple rule
If you can’t verify it, don’t write it as fact.

## Plain article frame
You could write it like this:

**The team played a school game and had trouble with spacing and passing. The game stayed close, but I would leave out the score and any specific details until they are confirmed.**

If you want, paste your notes and I can sort them into:
- **safe to use**
- **needs checking**
- **leave out**

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s closer. But I’d still want it a little more usable for actual writing, not just rules.

Like:
- **safe to use** = what I saw myself, or what I checked and trust
- **needs checking** = maybe true, but not ready for print
- **leave out** = anything I can’t back up

And for newspaper style, I’d keep it plain. No extra drama, no guessing. If the notes are thin, I’d rather have a short clean paragraph than try to fill gaps.

If you want, sort my notes into those 3 buckets and I’ll know what I can actually write.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — that’s the right standard for school newspaper style.

Paste your notes and I’ll sort each item into:

- **Safe to use** = you saw it yourself, or you checked it and trust it
- **Needs checking** = might be true, but not ready for print
- **Leave out** = can’t back it up yet

I’ll keep it plain and practical, and if you want, I can also turn the **safe** items into a short clean paragraph you could actually use.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah — here are my notes, but I’m keeping it strict:

- Tuesday afternoon
- school soccer game
- team looked rushed
- passing was messy
- spacing got kind of sloppy
- maybe 3-1, but I only saw that in one place
- one player scored twice, maybe Ava, but I’m not sure
- I think it was at home, but I didn’t verify that

Sort those into the 3 buckets, and if something is shaky, just put it in needs checking or leave it out. I’d rather be safe than wrong.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the strict sort:

## Safe to use
- **school soccer game**
- **team looked rushed**
- **passing was messy**
- **spacing got kind of sloppy**

These are fine **if they’re your own observations** from watching the game.

## Needs checking
- **Tuesday afternoon**
- **maybe 3-1, but I only saw that in one place**
- **one player scored twice, maybe Ava**
- **I think it was at home**

These might be true, but they’re **not ready for print** yet.

## Leave out
- Nothing here has to be left out completely yet, as long as you’re willing to keep the shaky details out for now and verify them later.

## What you can safely write right now
A plain version could be:

**The school soccer team played in a game and looked rushed at times. Passing was messy, and spacing on the field got sloppy.**

If you want, I can also turn the **safe** notes into a slightly fuller newspaper-style paragraph without adding anything unverified.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s basically right, but I’d be a little stricter on the date/home stuff. If I didn’t verify it, I’d keep it out of print for now, not just in “needs checking.”

So I’d sort it like this:

**Safe to use**
- school soccer game
- team looked rushed
- passing was messy
- spacing got kind of sloppy

**Needs checking**
- Tuesday afternoon
- maybe 3-1, but I only saw that in one place
- one player scored twice, maybe Ava
- I think it was at home

**Leave out**
- nothing necessarily has to be thrown away forever, but I wouldn’t use the shaky stuff as fact yet

If I was writing it, I’d only use the first four and keep it plain. Like:

**The school soccer team played in a game and looked rushed at times. Passing was messy, and spacing got sloppy.**

That feels clean enough for newspaper style. If you want, I can help make it sound a tiny bit more like an actual article without adding any extra facts.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — that’s the right call.

Your strict version is the safest one for school newspaper style:

## Safe to use
- school soccer game
- team looked rushed
- passing was messy
- spacing got kind of sloppy

## Needs checking
- Tuesday afternoon
- maybe 3-1
- one player scored twice, maybe Ava
- I think it was at home

## Leave out for now
- anything shaky you can’t back up yet

And your draft sentence is clean and usable:

**The school soccer team played in a game and looked rushed at times. Passing was messy, and spacing got sloppy.**

If you want, I can make that sound just a little more like a real newspaper sentence without adding any new facts.

user_simulator.text: Yeah, that’s good. Clean, plain, not trying to invent anything. That’s the standard I want for this kind of thing.
